title: Improving std::simd::swizzle_dyn | Sergey "Shnatsel" Davidoff
excerpt: Fearless SIMD recently received a pull request that started like this: Swizzles are a whole can of worm and I do not intend to figure out how we should implement this generically right now. 😄
content_html:
Fearless SIMD recently received a pull request that started like this:

Swizzles are a whole can of worm and I do not intend to figure out how we should implement this generically right now. 😄

The pull request author only wanted to convert RGBA image layouts, e.g. RGBA <-> BGRA, so we solved the immediate need by implementing a simpler version that shuffles bytes within 128-bit blocks. That way we can trivially support this operation even on 512-bit vectors on 128-bit hardware, and 512-bit hardware still gets to utilize its full potential with native instructions.

But that made me wonder: how does std::simd implement arbitrary swizzles that aren't confined to 128-bit blocks? Turns out the answer is: poorly.

Over the course of this article we're going to fix some of that.

What is a swizzle?

A swizzle, or a shuffle, rearranges elements within an array.

For example, if I have the input array [A,D,F,I,M,R,S,T] and apply the swizzle mask [2,0,6,7,6,3,4,1], I get [F,A,S,T,S,I,M,D].

The dyn part indicates that the swizzle mask is dynamic (not hardcoded), so you could supply the swizzle mask [6,4,0,5,7,0,6,6] at runtime to get a different word.

This example uses only 8 bytes, or 64 bits. In practice hardware implements shuffles on vector sizes from 128 to 512 bits, and so does std::simd.

Understanding the implementation

On a high level, the current implementation of std::simd::swizzle_dyn is very simple: use the native hardware operation if available, otherwise give up and move bytes one by one.

Even when hardware shuffles for a given size are available, swizzle_dyn doesn't always map to the hardware cleanly. It promises that the values for out-of-bounds indices will be set to 0, while x86 hardware shuffle just lets them wrap, so swizzle_dyn has to do extra work to uphold this guarantee.

Wait a minute... what is this warning in its documentation?

Note that the current implementation is selected during build-time of the standard library, so cargo build -Zbuild-std may be necessary to unlock better performance, especially for larger vectors. A planned compiler improvement will enable using #[target_feature] instead.

Uh oh.

The black sheep of std::simd

Rust uses LLVM for optimizing your code and turning into something CPUs can execute. And LLVM doesn't like dealing with platform-specific operations. It's much easier to implement an operation like "add two SIMD vectors" generically, run all optimizations on this generic form, and then select the appropriate instructions for a given CPU later.

Most std::simd operations simply emit the generic form such as "add two SIMD vectors", which makes the implementation remarkably straightforward.

But swizzle_dyn does not. And the implementation looks something like this:

#[cfg(target_feature = "neon"))]
unsafe fn armv7_neon_swizzle_u8x16(bytes: Simd<u8, 16>, idxs: Simd<u8, 16>) -> Simd<u8, 16> {
    use core::arch::arm::{uint8x8x2_t, vcombine_u8, vget_high_u8, vget_low_u8, vtbl2_u8};
    unsafe {
        let bytes = uint8x8x2_t(vget_low_u8(bytes.into()), vget_high_u8(bytes.into()));
        let lo = vtbl2_u8(bytes, vget_low_u8(idxs.into()));
        let hi = vtbl2_u8(bytes, vget_high_u8(idxs.into()));
        vcombine_u8(lo, hi).into()
    }
}

I've seen that before! That's no LLVM magic, even if it's completely inscrutable. Those are plain old SIMD intrinsics!

The problem is those platform-specific intrinsics are under #[cfg(target_feature = ...))]. It's that cfg that kills performance, because it gets resolved very early in the build process, before the context from which the function is called is considered. fearless_simd and every other SIMD crate that isn't called wide has a solution for this, but std::simd doesn't, and it can't just adopt the ecosystem solution without a large change to the API.

The warning in documentation doesn't convey the full extent of the problem. Not only is -Z build-std necessary, you also have to pass RUSTFLAGS=-C target-cpu= to get decent performance. But if you do that and you run the program on a CPU that's older than the one you selected, the program will crash. The typical solution is selecting the best implementation at runtime via crates such as multiversion, but that does not work for swizzle_dyn, even with -Z build-std.

And let me be clear: I'm not a compiler engineer. I'm not the one who's going to build whatever compiler improvement is planned to fix this.

But I am a fearless_simd maintainer, and I already know more about SIMD intrinsics than ever wanted to. So there is plenty here that I can improve.

No NEON?

The first thing that stuck out to me when reading the swizzle_dyn code is that it only implements 128-bit swizzles on ARM NEON, even though NEON does have hardware instructions for larger swizzles.

It turned out that std::simd isn't actually developed as part of the Rust standard library - it has its own repository where development happens, and the result is periodically synced into the standard library. And the missing NEON implementations were already implemented there.

That wasn't at all apparent when browsing the code. I nearly wasted a lot of time making pull requests for an outdated version of the code in the wrong repository. Adding headers to synced-in files that specify how to contribute to them would save other people in my position a lot of time.

Doubling the vector width

Let's say we want to execute [A,D,F,I,M,R,S,T].swizzle_dyn([2,0,6,7,6,3,4,1]) but all we have is half-width vectors. swizzle_dyn setting out-of-bounds elements to zero is going to help us here.

To process the first half, [2,0,6,7], we run [A,D,F,I].swizzle_dyn([2,0,6,7]) and [M,R,S,T].swizzle_dyn([-2,-4,2,3]), which gives us [F,A,0,0] and [0,0,S,T]. Then we combine these two intermediate results with a cheap bitwise OR, et voilà! We get [F,A,S,T]! Now repeat the same for the second half, and we're done!

It is rare that a portability guarantee actually helps optimize something instead of incurring additional work, but this time we're in luck!

This takes four shuffles instead of one, but it's still much faster than moving bytes around one at a time. I've measured a 4x performance improvement from implementing this on AVX2 for 512-bit vectors inside fearless_simd.

This is now merged into std::simd, and will be available in the next release of fearless_simd too.

Using this approach for 4x the vector size would require 16 shuffles, so I don't think it's worth it, but it might still be worth benchmarking.

Optimizing AVX2

AVX2 is weird. It has 256-bit vectors, but most operations process 128-bit halves independently. That's fine for most operations, but problematic for a swizzle that may move values across those halves. So the AVX2 implementation needs two separate steps: shuffling within 128-bit blocks, and then using a trick similar to what we've just done.

Another complication is that swizzle_dyn promises that values for out-of-bounds indices will be set to 0, which x86 hardware shuffles don't do, so we have to implement that in software.

The current implementation looks like this:

use x86::_mm256_permute2x128_si256 as avx2_cross_shuffle;
use x86::_mm256_shuffle_epi8 as avx2_half_pshufb;

let mid = Simd::splat(16u8);
let high = mid + mid;

// This is ordering sensitive, and LLVM will order these how you put them.
// Most AVX2 impls use ~5 "ports", and only 1 or 2 are capable of permutes.
// But the "compose" step will lower to ops that can also use at least 1 other port.
// So this tries to break up permutes so composition flows through "open" ports.
// Comparative benches should be done on multiple AVX2 CPUs before reordering this

let hihi = avx2_cross_shuffle::<0x11>(bytes.into(), bytes.into());
let hi_shuf = Simd::from(avx2_half_pshufb(
    hihi,        // duplicate the vector's top half
    idxs.into(), // so that using only 4 bits of an index still picks bytes 16-31
));
// A zero-fill during the compose step gives the "all-Neon-like" OOB-is-0 semantics
let compose = idxs.simd_lt(high).select(hi_shuf, Simd::splat(0));
let lolo = avx2_cross_shuffle::<0x00>(bytes.into(), bytes.into());
let lo_shuf = Simd::from(avx2_half_pshufb(lolo, idxs.into()));
// Repeat, then pick indices < 16, overwriting indices 0-15 from previous compose step
let compose = idxs.simd_lt(mid).select(lo_shuf, compose);
compose

That's pretty clever. The core insight is this:

CPUs have a bunch of different hardware curcuits, and the circuit that handles shuffles is entirely separate from a circuit that handles arithmetic, so you can run them both at the same time. This is known as "instruction-level parallelism".

The above code is carefully tuned to spread the work across different circuits in such a way that it doesn't pile too much work on a single circuit (aka "port") and take advantage of this parallelism to speed things up.

You can measure the effectiveness of this by viewing the resulting assembly with cargo-show-asm and feeding the result to llvm-mca (Machine Code Analyzer), which models the execution of the given assembly on a specific CPU model accounting for all of these effects.

According to llvm-mca, spreading operations across different kinds of ports is important on the early Intel CPUs with AVX2, namely Haswell and Broadwell. On the other end of the spectrum, recent Intel Tiger Lake and all AMD Zen generations are so stuffed with all kinds of circuits that they favor doing less work overall, and you don't have to worry about overwhelming one circuit anymore. Intel Skylake is indifferent, dealing with all formulations in an equally mediocre fashion.

It's easy to get distracted arguing over which platform should be prioritized and optimized for. On one hand, recent CPUs are much more common these days, so optimizing for them benefits more people. On the other, the older CPUs are underpowered by today's standards and need all the help they can get.

We can sidestep that entirely by using a clever algorithm that eliminates the compare-select steps, which improves performance on recent CPUs without regressing it on antiquated ones:

use x86::_mm256_permute2x128_si256 as avx2_cross_shuffle;
use x86::_mm256_shuffle_epi8 as avx2_half_pshufb;

let lolo = avx2_cross_shuffle::<0x00>(bytes.into(), bytes.into());
let hihi = avx2_cross_shuffle::<0x11>(bytes.into(), bytes.into());

// Adding 0x60 preserves the low nibble and bit 4 for valid
// indices 0..=31. Larger indices get their high bit set, so
// VPSHUFB supplies the required out-of-bounds zeroing.
let control = x86::_mm256_adds_epu8(idxs.into(), x86::_mm256_set1_epi8(0x60));

// Move index bit 4 into each byte's sign bit for VPBLENDVB.
let select_high = x86::_mm256_slli_epi16::<3>(control);
let from_low = avx2_half_pshufb(lolo, control);
let from_high = avx2_half_pshufb(hihi, control);
x86::_mm256_blendv_epi8(from_low, from_high, select_high).into()

llvm-mca shows no change on Haswell and Broadwell, but Zen 1, Zen 2 and Tiger Lake show a 23% improvement, and a 7-20% improvement on Zen 3. Skylake is unchanged for the 256-bit shuffle, but the decomposition of a 512-bit shuffle into four 256-bit ones improves by 20%.

I'm glossing over the throughput vs latency distinction here, since this formulation improves both.

This change is currently under review in std::simd, and fearless_simd will ship with this formulation from the start.

Was it worth it?

Performance of a single SIMD operation in isolation does not necessarily translate to improvements in algorithms using it.

For example, AVX2 only has 16 SIMD registers to hold data the CPU can directly operate on. If your operation runs faster but uses a lot more registers, the entire algorithm it's in may run out of registers and have to spill data onto the stack, which adds costly load/store instructions, which negates the gains or even slows down the entire algorithm!

We need to measure performance of some plausible code that uses 512-bit swizzles. 512 bits is 64 bytes, so base64 encoding/decoding is a natural fit.

I'm not aware of any shuffle-based Rust implementations of base64 that I could simply reuse. But it's easy enough to have an LLM conjure a prototype, for educational purposes only. Here's how it performs with different shuffle implementations:

scalar fallback:   1.6 GiB/s encode, 0.9 GiB/s decode
AVX2 double-width: 8.2 GiB/s encode, 5.5 GiB/s decode
AVX2 dw optimized: 9.4 GiB/s encode, 5.9 GiB/s decode

That's a ~6x improvement just from the work described in this article!

Let's see how it stacks up against Sunny Young's base64 using std::simd, which uses a lot of clever tricks and you should totally go read their article about it:

AVX2 vb64: 5.9 GiB/s encode, 5.7 GiB/s decode

Brute-forcing the work with emulated swizzles and landing in the same ballpark as an implementation that carefully avoids them using a clever perfect hash is not too shabby! And ours have the added benefit of swapping out the base64 alphabet at will, while vb64's perfect hash is tied to the standard base64 alphabet.

A production implementation without SIMD runs at 2.7 GiB/s encode and 2.3 GiB/s decode, so SIMD is well worth it. Our implementation on an AVX-512 CPU runs at ~13 GiB/s, while vb64 doesn't benefit from AVX-512 nearly as much. A state-of-the art AVX-512 implementation translated to safe Rust runs at 35 GiB/s encode and 20 GiB/s decode, so our prototype is not optimal, but still lets us see improvements to individual operations benefit a larger algorithm.

I've run these measurements on my Zen 4 workstation on fearless_simd, because the contributing guide for std::simd doesn't say how to benchmark your changes.

Is fearless_simd better?

For this one specific operation, swizzle_dyn, yes. The implementations are now identical in both std::simd and fearless_simd, but the latter doesn't require -Z build-std and can multiversion this function just fine.

But when std::simd is playing to its strengths, it is really hard to beat or even match.

For example, std::simd::swizzle that accepts indices known at compile time translates directly into an LLVM "shuffle vector" operation, and LLVM selects the best formulation for your CPU out of a long list of special cases.

Replicating this on stable Rust would require a lot of work, and either a lot of const evaluation or a procedural macro, both of which would negatively impact build time. To the best of my knowledge, nobody has even attempted this yet.

That said, LLVM is not perfect. For example, I've found that it destroys performance of certain AVX-512 shuffles by turning them into a lot of SSE operations instead of just keeping it as a native AVX-512 shuffle. I've reported this as an issue in Rust, then a Rust standard library maintainer reported it to LLVM and attempted to fix it, and that led to a whole bunch of improvements made by LLVM developers. This work will ship in LLVM 23.

I've already uncovered another LLVM inefficiency, but the PR to fix it just sends LLVM into an infinite loop. So here's a fun debugging puzzle if you're looking for one!

LLVM issues affect std::simd, all other SIMD crates, and even other languages like C and C++, so fixing them is always a big win.

Takeaways

So here's what I've learned:

  • std::simd::swizzle_dyn is even less useful than it appears, since multiversioning doesn't work on it.
  • std::simd on nightly is outdated compared to the actual development state.
  • The contributing documentation for std::simd is poor:
    • It's easy to open PRs for an outdated version in the wrong repository.
    • There's no documented way to disassemble or benchmark your changes.
  • LLVM can mangle perfectly optimal SIMD code, even if you use intrinsics that are supposed to lower to a specific instruction. Not just in Rust, in C and C++ too!

And, well, std::simd::swizzle_dyn will be up to 6x faster once this work gets synced into the standard library. You're welcome!

The implementations are now identical in both std::simd and fearless_simd, but the latter doesn't require -Z build-std and can multiversion this function just fine.But when std::simd is playing to its strengths, it is really hard to beat or even match.For example, std::simd::swizzle that accepts indices known at compile time translates directly into an LLVM "shuffle vector" operation, and LLVM selects the best formulation for your CPU out of a long list of special cases.Replicating this on stable Rust would require a lot of work, and either a lot of const evaluation or a procedural macro, both of which would negatively impact build time. To the best of my knowledge, nobody has even attempted this yet.That said, LLVM is not perfect. For example, I've found that it destroys performance of certain AVX-512 shuffles by turning them into a lot of SSE operations instead of just keeping it as a native AVX-512 shuffle. I've reported this as an issue in Rust, then a Rust standard library maintainer reported it to LLVM and attempted to fix it, and that led to a whole bunch of improvements made by LLVM developers. This work will ship in LLVM 23.I've already uncovered another LLVM inefficiency, but the PR to fix it just sends LLVM into an infinite loop. So here's a fun debugging puzzle if you're looking for one!LLVM issues affect std::simd, all other SIMD crates, and even other languages like C and C++, so fixing them is always a big win.TakeawaysSo here's what I've learned:std::simd::swizzle_dyn is even less useful than it appears, since multiversioning doesn't work on it.std::simd on nightly is outdated compared to the actual development state.The contributing documentation for std::simd is poor: It's easy to open PRs for an outdated version in the wrong repository.There's no documented way to disassemble or benchmark your changes.LLVM can mangle perfectly optimal SIMD code, even if you use intrinsics that are supposed to lower to a specific instruction. Not just in Rust, in C and C++ too!And, well, std::simd::swizzle_dyn will be up to 6x faster once this work gets synced into the standard library. You're welcome!

blocks.html_en: On a high level, the current implementation of std::simd::swizzle_dyn is very simple: use the native hardware operation if available, otherwise give up and move bytes one by one.

It promises that the values for out-of-bounds indices will be set to 0, while x86 hardware shuffle just lets them wrap, so swizzle_dyn has to do extra work to uphold this guarantee.

blocks.html_en: Uh oh.
The black sheep of std::simd

sentences.en: The black sheep of std::simd
sentences.zh: std::simd 的害群之马

blocks.html_en: Rust uses LLVM for optimizing your code and turning into something CPUs can execute. And LLVM doesn't like dealing with platform-specific operations. It's much easier to implement an operation like "add two SIMD vectors" generically, run all optimizations on this generic form, and then select the appropriate instructions for a given CPU later.

sentences.en: Rust uses LLVM for optimizing your code and turning into something CPUs can execute.
sentences.zh: Rust 使用 LLVM 来优化代码并将其转换为 CPU 可以执行的代码。

sentences.en: And LLVM doesn't like dealing with platform-specific operations.
sentences.zh: 而且 LLVM 不喜欢处理特定于平台的操作。

sentences.en: It's much easier to implement an operation like "add two SIMD vectors" generically, run all optimizations on this generic form, and then select the appropriate instructions for a given CPU later.
sentences.zh: 通用地实现“添加两个 SIMD 向量”之类的操作，在此通用形式上运行所有优化，然后稍后为给定的 CPU 选择适当的指令要容易得多。

blocks.html_en: Most std::simd operations simply emit the generic form such as "add two SIMD vectors", which makes the implementation remarkably straightforward.

sentences.en: Most std::simd operations simply emit the generic form such as "add two SIMD vectors", which makes the implementation remarkably straightforward.
sentences.zh: 大多数 std::simd 操作只是发出通用形式，例如“添加两个 SIMD 向量”，这使得实现非常简单。

blocks.html_en: But swizzle_dyn does not. And the implementation looks something like this:

blocks.html_en: I've seen that before! That's no LLVM magic, even if it's completely inscrutable. Those are plain old SIMD intrinsics!

sentences.en: I've seen that before!
sentences.zh: 我以前见过！

sentences.en: That's no LLVM magic, even if it's completely inscrutable.
sentences.zh: 这并不是 LLVM 的魔法，即使它完全难以理解。

sentences.en: Those are plain old SIMD intrinsics!
sentences.zh: 这些是普通的旧 SIMD 内在函数！

blocks.html_en: The problem is those platform-specific intrinsics are under #[cfg(target_feature = ...))]. It's that cfg that kills performance, because it gets resolved very early in the build process, before the context from which the function is called is considered. fearless_simd and every other SIMD crate that isn't called wide has a solution for this, but std::simd doesn't, and it can't just adopt the ecosystem solution without a large change to the API.

sentences.en: The problem is those platform-specific intrinsics are under #[cfg(target_feature = ...))].
sentences.zh: 问题是那些特定于平台的内在函数位于 #[cfg(target_feature = ...))] 下。

sentences.en: It's that cfg that kills performance, because it gets resolved very early in the build process, before the context from which the function is called is considered.
sentences.zh: 正是该 cfg 影响了性能，因为它在构建过程的早期就得到了解决，在考虑调用该函数的上下文之前。

sentences.en: fearless_simd and every other SIMD crate that isn't called wide has a solution for this, but std::simd doesn't, and it can't just adopt the ecosystem solution without a large change to the API.
sentences.zh: fearless_simd 和其他所有不被称为 Wide 的 SIMD 包都有一个解决方案，但 std::simd 没有，并且它不能在不对 API 进行重大更改的情况下直接采用生态系统解决方案。

blocks.html_en: The warning in documentation doesn't convey the full extent of the problem. Not only is -Z build-std necessary, you also have to pass RUSTFLAGS=-C target-cpu= to get decent performance. But if you do that and you run the program on a CPU that's older than the one you selected, the program will crash. The typical solution is selecting the best implementation at runtime via crates such as multiversion, but that does not work for swizzle_dyn, even with -Z build-std.

blocks.html_en: And let me be clear: I'm not a compiler engineer. I'm not the one who's going to build whatever compiler improvement is planned to fix this.

blocks.html_en: But I am a fearless_simd maintainer, and I already know more about SIMD intrinsics than ever wanted to. So there is plenty here that I can improve.

blocks.html_en: The first thing that stuck out to me when reading the swizzle_dyn code is that it only implements 128-bit swizzles on ARM NEON, even though NEON does have hardware instructions for larger swizzles.

sentences.en: The first thing that stuck out to me when reading the swizzle_dyn code is that it only implements 128-bit swizzles on ARM NEON, even though NEON does have hardware instructions for larger swizzles.

blocks.html_en: It turned out that std::simd isn't actually developed as part of the Rust standard library - it has its own repository where development happens, and the result is periodically synced into the standard library. And the missing NEON implementations were already implemented there.

sentences.en: Doubling the vector width
sentences.zh: 将矢量宽度加倍

blocks.html_en: Let's say we want to execute [A,D,F,I,M,R,S,T].swizzle_dyn([2,0,6,7,6,3,4,1]) but all we have is half-width vectors. swizzle_dyn setting out-of-bounds elements to zero is going to help us here.

sentences.en: Let's say we want to execute [A,D,F,I,M,R,S,T].swizzle_dyn([2,0,6,7,6,3,4,1]) but all we have is half-width vectors.
sentences.zh: 假设我们想要执行 [A,D,F,I,M,R,S,T].swizzle_dyn([2,0,6,7,6,3,4,1]) 但我们拥有的只是半角向量。

sentences.en: swizzle_dyn setting out-of-bounds elements to zero is going to help us here.
sentences.zh: swizzle_dyn 将越界元素设置为零将对我们有所帮助。

blocks.html_en: To process the first half, [2,0,6,7], we run [A,D,F,I].swizzle_dyn([2,0,6,7]) and [M,R,S,T].swizzle_dyn([-2,-4,2,3]), which gives us [F,A,0,0] and [0,0,S,T]. Then we combine these two intermediate results with a cheap bitwise OR, et voilà! We get [F,A,S,T]! Now repeat the same for the second half, and we're done!

sentences.en: To process the first half, [2,0,6,7], we run [A,D,F,I].swizzle_dyn([2,0,6,7]) and [M,R,S,T].swizzle_dyn([-2,-4,2,3]), which gives us [F,A,0,0] and [0,0,S,T].
sentences.zh: 为了处理前半部分 [2,0,6,7]，我们运行 [A,D,F,I].swizzle_dyn([2,0,6,7]) 和 [M,R,S,T].swizzle_dyn([-2,-4,2,3])，这给出了 [F,A,0,0] 和 [0,0,S,T]。

sentences.zh: 然后我们用一个简单的按位“或”组合这两个中间结果，瞧！

sentences.en: Now repeat the same for the second half, and we're done!

blocks.html_en: It is rare that a portability guarantee actually helps optimize something instead of incurring additional work, but this time we're in luck!

sentences.en: It is rare that a portability guarantee actually helps optimize something instead of incurring additional work, but this time we're in luck!
sentences.zh: 可移植性保证实际上很少会帮助优化某些东西而不是招致额外的工作，但这一次我们很幸运！

blocks.html_en: This takes four shuffles instead of one, but it's still much faster than moving bytes around one at a time. I've measured a 4x performance improvement from implementing this on AVX2 for 512-bit vectors inside fearless_simd.

sentences.en: This takes four shuffles instead of one, but it's still much faster than moving bytes around one at a time.
sentences.zh: 这需要四次洗牌而不是一次，但它仍然比一次移动一个字节要快得多。

sentences.en: I've measured a 4x performance improvement from implementing this on AVX2 for 512-bit vectors inside fearless_simd.
sentences.zh: 我测量到，通过在 AVX2 上为 fearless_simd 内的 512 位向量实现此方法，性能提高了 4 倍。

blocks.html_en: This is now merged into std::simd, and will be available in the next release of fearless_simd too.

sentences.en: This is now merged into std::simd, and will be available in the next release of fearless_simd too.
sentences.zh: 现在它已合并到 std::simd 中，并且也将在下一个版本的 fearless_simd 中提供。

blocks.html_en: Using this approach for 4x the vector size would require 16 shuffles, so I don't think it's worth it, but it might still be worth benchmarking.

sentences.en: Using this approach for 4x the vector size would require 16 shuffles, so I don't think it's worth it, but it might still be worth benchmarking.
sentences.zh: 使用这种方法 4 倍向量大小将需要 16 次洗牌，所以我认为这不值得，但它可能仍然值得进行基准测试。

blocks.html_en: Optimizing AVX2

sentences.en: Optimizing AVX2
sentences.zh: 优化 AVX2

blocks.html_en: AVX2 is weird. It has 256-bit vectors, but most operations process 128-bit halves independently. That's fine for most operations, but problematic for a swizzle that may move values across those halves. So the AVX2 implementation needs two separate steps: shuffling within 128-bit blocks, and then using a trick similar to what we've just done.

sentences.en: AVX2 is weird.
sentences.zh: AVX2 很奇怪。

sentences.en: It has 256-bit vectors, but most operations process 128-bit halves independently.
sentences.zh: 它有 256 位向量，但大多数操作独立处理 128 位向量。

sentences.en: That's fine for most operations, but problematic for a swizzle that may move values across those halves.
sentences.zh: 这对于大多数操作来说都很好，但对于可能在这两个部分之间移动值的混合来说是有问题的。

sentences.en: So the AVX2 implementation needs two separate steps: shuffling within 128-bit blocks, and then using a trick similar to what we've just done.
sentences.zh: 因此 AVX2 实现需要两个单独的步骤：在 128 位块内进行混洗，然后使用与我们刚刚所做的类似的技巧。

blocks.html_en: Another complication is that swizzle_dyn promises that values for out-of-bounds indices will be set to 0, which x86 hardware shuffles don't do, so we have to implement that in software.

sentences.en: Another complication is that swizzle_dyn promises that values for out-of-bounds indices will be set to 0, which x86 hardware shuffles don't do, so we have to implement that in software.
sentences.zh: 另一个复杂之处是 swizzle_dyn 承诺越界索引的值将设置为 0，而 x86 硬件洗牌不会这样做，因此我们必须在软件中实现这一点。

blocks.html_en: The current implementation looks like this:

blocks.html_en: CPUs have a bunch of different hardware curcuits, and the circuit that handles shuffles is entirely separate from a circuit that handles arithmetic, so you can run them both at the same time. This is known as "instruction-level parallelism".

sentences.en: CPUs have a bunch of different hardware curcuits, and the circuit that handles shuffles is entirely separate from a circuit that handles arithmetic, so you can run them both at the same time.

sentences.en: This is known as "instruction-level parallelism".
sentences.zh: 这称为“指令级并行性”。

blocks.html_en: The above code is carefully tuned to spread the work across different circuits in such a way that it doesn't pile too much work on a single circuit (aka "port") and take advantage of this parallelism to speed things up.

sentences.en: The above code is carefully tuned to spread the work across different circuits in such a way that it doesn't pile too much work on a single circuit (aka "port") and take advantage of this parallelism to speed things up.

blocks.html_en: You can measure the effectiveness of this by viewing the resulting assembly with cargo-show-asm and feeding the result to llvm-mca (Machine Code Analyzer), which models the execution of the given assembly on a specific CPU model accounting for all of these effects.

blocks.html_en: According to llvm-mca, spreading operations across different kinds of ports is important on the early Intel CPUs with AVX2, namely Haswell and Broadwell. On the other end of the spectrum, recent Intel Tiger Lake and all AMD Zen generations are so stuffed with all kinds of circuits that they favor doing less work overall, and you don't have to worry about overwhelming one circuit anymore. Intel Skylake is indifferent, dealing with all formulations in an equally mediocre fashion.

content_html_zh:
Fearless SIMD 最近收到了一个拉取请求，其开头如下：

Swizzles 是一整套蠕虫病毒，我现在不打算弄清楚我们应该如何实现这一点。 😄

拉取请求作者只想转换 RGBA 图像布局，例如RGBA <-> BGRA，因此我们通过实现一个更简单的版本（在 128 位块内对字节进行混洗）解决了当前的需求。 这样，即使在 128 位硬件上的 512 位向量上，我们也可以轻松支持此操作，并且 512 位硬件仍然可以通过本机指令充分利用其潜力。

但这让我想知道：std::simd 如何实现不限于 128 位块的任意混合？ 结果答案是：很差。

在本文中，我们将修复其中的一些问题。

什么是调酒？

swizzle 或 shuffle 会重新排列数组中的元素。

例如，如果我有输入数组 [A,D,F,I,M,R,S,T] 并应用混合掩码 [2,0,6,7,6,3,4,1]，我会得到 [F,A,S,T,S,I,M,D]。

dyn 部分表示 swizzle 掩码是动态的（不是硬编码的），因此您可以在运行时提供 swizzle 掩码 [6,4,0,5,7,0,6,6] 以获得不同的单词。

此示例仅使用 8 个字节，即 64 位。 实际上，硬件对 128 到 512 位的向量大小实现了洗牌，std::simd 也是如此。

了解实施

从较高的层面来看， std::simd::swizzle_dyn 的当前实现非常简单：如果可用，则使用本机硬件操作，否则放弃并逐个移动字节。

即使给定大小的硬件洗牌可用，swizzle_dyn 也并不总是干净地映射到硬件。 它承诺越界索引的值将设置为 0，而 x86 硬件 shuffle 只是让它们换行，因此 swizzle_dyn 必须做额外的工作来维护这一保证。

等一下... 其文档中的这个警告是什么？

请注意，当前的实现是在标准库的构建期间选择的，因此可能需要 Cargo build -Zbuild-std 来解锁更好的性能，特别是对于较大的向量。 计划中的编译器改进将允许使用 #[target_feature] 代替。

Uh oh.

std::simd 的害群之马

Rust 使用 LLVM 来优化代码并将其转换为 CPU 可以执行的代码。 而且 LLVM 不喜欢处理特定于平台的操作。 通用地实现“添加两个 SIMD 向量”之类的操作，在此通用形式上运行所有优化，然后稍后为给定的 CPU 选择适当的指令要容易得多。

大多数 std::simd 操作只是发出通用形式，例如“添加两个 SIMD 向量”，这使得实现非常简单。

但 swizzle_dyn 没有。 实现看起来像这样：

#[cfg(target_feature = "neon"))]
unsafe fn armv7_neon_swizzle_u8x16(bytes: Simd<u8, 16>, idxs: Simd<u8, 16>) -> Simd<u8, 16> {
    use core::arch::arm::{uint8x8x2_t, vcombine_u8, vget_high_u8, vget_low_u8, vtbl2_u8};
    unsafe {
        let bytes = uint8x8x2_t(vget_low_u8(bytes.into()), vget_high_u8(bytes.into()));
        let lo = vtbl2_u8(bytes, vget_low_u8(idxs.into()));
        let hi = vtbl2_u8(bytes, vget_high_u8(idxs.into()));
        vcombine_u8(lo, hi).into()
    }
}

我以前见过！ 这并不是 LLVM 的魔法，即使它完全难以理解。 这些是普通的旧 SIMD 内在函数！

问题是那些特定于平台的内在函数位于 #[cfg(target_feature = ...))] 下。 正是该 cfg 影响了性能，因为它在构建过程的早期就得到了解决，在考虑调用该函数的上下文之前。 fearless_simd 和其他所有不被称为 Wide 的 SIMD 包都有一个解决方案，但 std::simd 没有，并且它不能在不对 API 进行重大更改的情况下直接采用生态系统解决方案。

文档中的警告并未传达问题的全部范围。 不仅 -Z build-std 是必要的，您还必须通过 RUSTFLAGS=-C target-cpu= 才能获得不错的性能。 但是，如果您这样做并且在比您选择的 CPU 更旧的 CPU 上运行该程序，则该程序将会崩溃。 典型的解决方案是通过诸如多版本之类的包在运行时选择最佳实现，但这对于 swizzle_dyn 不起作用，即使使用 -Z build-std 也是如此。

让我澄清一下：我不是编译器工程师。 我不是那个打算构建任何编译器改进来解决这个问题的人。

但我是一个 fearless_simd 维护者，而且我对 SIMD 内在函数的了解已经比以前想要的更多了。 所以这里有很多我可以改进的地方。

没有霓虹灯？

在阅读 swizzle_dyn 代码时，我首先注意到的是，它仅在 ARM NEON 上实现 128 位 swizzle，尽管 NEON 确实具有用于更大 swizzles 的硬件指令。

事实证明， std::simd 实际上并不是作为 Rust 标准库的一部分开发的 - 它有自己的存储库，用于进行开发，并且结果会定期同步到标准库中。 缺少的 NEON 实现已经在那里实现了。

当浏览代码时，这一点并不明显。 我几乎浪费了很多时间对错误存储库中过时版本的代码发出拉取请求。 向同步文件添加标头，指定如何为其做出贡献，这将为处于我位置的其他人节省大量时间。

将矢量宽度加倍

假设我们想要执行 [A,D,F,I,M,R,S,T].swizzle_dyn([2,0,6,7,6,3,4,1]) 但我们拥有的只是半角向量。 swizzle_dyn 将越界元素设置为零将对我们有所帮助。

为了处理前半部分 [2,0,6,7]，我们运行 [A,D,F,I].swizzle_dyn([2,0,6,7]) 和 [M,R,S,T].swizzle_dyn([-2,-4,2,3])，这给出了 [F,A,0,0] 和 [0,0,S,T]。 然后我们用一个简单的按位“或”组合这两个中间结果，瞧！ 我们得到[F,A,S,T]！ 现在在下半场重复同样的事情，我们就完成了！

可移植性保证实际上很少会帮助优化某些东西而不是招致额外的工作，但这一次我们很幸运！

这需要四次洗牌而不是一次，但它仍然比一次移动一个字节要快得多。 我测量到，通过在 AVX2 上为 fearless_simd 内的 512 位向量实现此方法，性能提高了 4 倍。

现在它已合并到 std::simd 中，并且也将在下一个版本的 fearless_simd 中提供。

使用这种方法 4 倍向量大小将需要 16 次洗牌，所以我认为这不值得，但它可能仍然值得进行基准测试。

优化 AVX2

AVX2 很奇怪。 它有 256 位向量，但大多数操作独立处理 128 位向量。 这对于大多数操作来说都很好，但对于可能在这两个部分之间移动值的混合来说是有问题的。 因此 AVX2 实现需要两个单独的步骤：在 128 位块内进行混洗，然后使用与我们刚刚所做的类似的技巧。

另一个复杂之处是 swizzle_dyn 承诺越界索引的值将设置为 0，而 x86 硬件洗牌不会这样做，因此我们必须在软件中实现这一点。

当前的实现如下所示：

use x86::_mm256_permute2x128_si256 as avx2_cross_shuffle;
use x86::_mm256_shuffle_epi8 as avx2_half_pshufb;

let mid = Simd::splat(16u8);
let high = mid + mid;

// This is ordering sensitive, and LLVM will order these how you put them.
// Most AVX2 impls use ~5 "ports", and only 1 or 2 are capable of permutes.
// But the "compose" step will lower to ops that can also use at least 1 other port.
// So this tries to break up permutes so composition flows through "open" ports.
// Comparative benches should be done on multiple AVX2 CPUs before reordering this

let hihi = avx2_cross_shuffle::<0x11>(bytes.into(), bytes.into());
let hi_shuf = Simd::from(avx2_half_pshufb(
    hihi,        // duplicate the vector's top half
    idxs.into(), // so that using only 4 bits of an index still picks bytes 16-31
));
// A zero-fill during the compose step gives the "all-Neon-like" OOB-is-0 semantics
let compose = idxs.simd_lt(high).select(hi_shuf, Simd::splat(0));
let lolo = avx2_cross_shuffle::<0x00>(bytes.into(), bytes.into());
let lo_shuf = Simd::from(avx2_half_pshufb(lolo, idxs.into()));
// Repeat, then pick indices < 16, overwriting indices 0-15 from previous compose step
let compose = idxs.simd_lt(mid).select(lo_shuf, compose);
compose

这非常聪明。 核心见解是这样的：

CPU 有许多不同的硬件电路，处理洗牌的电路与处理算术的电路完全分开，因此您可以同时运行它们。 这称为“指令级并行性”。

上面的代码经过仔细调整，可以将工作分散到不同的电路上，这样就不会在单个电路（也称为“端口”）上堆积太多工作，并利用这种并行性来加快速度。

您可以通过使用cargo-show-asm查看生成的程序集并将结果提供给llvm-mca（机器代码分析器）来衡量其有效性，该分析器会在考虑所有这些影响的特定CPU模型上对给定程序集的执行进行建模。

根据 llvm-mca 的说法，在具有 AVX2 的早期 Intel CPU（即 Haswell 和 Broadwell）上，跨不同类型的端口分散操作非常重要。 另一方面，最近的 Intel Tiger Lake 和所有 AMD Zen 代都充满了各种电路，因此它们倾向于整体减少工作量，您不必再担心某个电路不堪重负。 英特尔Skylake表现冷漠，以同样平庸的方式处理所有配方。

在争论应该优先考虑和优化哪个平台时，很容易分心。 一方面，最近的 CPU 更加常见，因此针对它们进行优化可以让更多的人受益。 另一方面，较旧的 CPU 无法满足当今的标准，需要获得所有的帮助。

我们可以通过使用一种巧妙的算法来完全回避这个问题，该算法消除了比较选择步骤，从而提高了最新 CPU 的性能，而不会降低过时 CPU 的性能：

use x86::_mm256_permute2x128_si256 as avx2_cross_shuffle;
use x86::_mm256_shuffle_epi8 as avx2_half_pshufb;

let lolo = avx2_cross_shuffle::<0x00>(bytes.into(), bytes.into());
let hihi = avx2_cross_shuffle::<0x11>(bytes.into(), bytes.into());

// Adding 0x60 preserves the low nibble and bit 4 for valid
// indices 0..=31. Larger indices get their high bit set, so
// VPSHUFB supplies the required out-of-bounds zeroing.
let control = x86::_mm256_adds_epu8(idxs.into(), x86::_mm256_set1_epi8(0x60));

// Move index bit 4 into each byte's sign bit for VPBLENDVB.
let select_high = x86::_mm256_slli_epi16::<3>(control);
let from_low = avx2_half_pshufb(lolo, control);
let from_high = avx2_half_pshufb(hihi, control);
x86::_mm256_blendv_epi8(from_low, from_high, select_high).into()

llvm-mca 在 Haswell 和 Broadwell 上没有变化，但 Zen 1、Zen 2 和 Tiger Lake 表现出 23% 的改进，比 Zen 3 表现出 7-20% 的改进。 Skylake 对于 256 位 shuffle 没有变化，但将 512 位 shuffle 分解为四个 256 位 shuffle 提高了 20%。

我在这里掩盖了吞吐量与延迟的区别，因为这个公式改善了两者。

此更改目前正在 std::simd 中进行审核，fearless_simd 将从一开始就附带此公式。

值得吗？

单独的 SIMD 操作的性能并不一定会转化为使用它的算法的改进。

例如，AVX2只有16个SIMD寄存器来保存CPU可以直接操作的数据。 如果您的操作运行速度更快，但使用更多寄存器，则其所在的整个算法可能会耗尽寄存器，并且必须将数据溢出到堆栈上，这会增加昂贵的加载/存储指令，从而抵消收益，甚至减慢整个算法！

我们需要测量一些使用 512 位 swizzle 的合理代码的性能。 512 位是 64 字节，因此 base64 编码/解码是很自然的选择。

我不知道有任何基于 shuffle 的 Base64 Rust 实现可以简单地重用。 但法学硕士很容易想出一个原型，仅用于教育目的。 以下是它在不同的 shuffle 实现中的执行情况：

scalar fallback:   1.6 GiB/s encode, 0.9 GiB/s decode
AVX2 double-width: 8.2 GiB/s encode, 5.5 GiB/s decode
AVX2 dw optimized: 9.4 GiB/s encode, 5.9 GiB/s decode

仅从本文中描述的工作来看，就实现了约 6 倍的改进！

让我们看看它如何与 Sunny Young 使用 std::simd 的 base64 相比，它使用了很多巧妙的技巧，你完全应该去阅读他们关于它的文章：

AVX2 vb64: 5.9 GiB/s encode, 5.7 GiB/s decode

使用模拟的 swizzles 进行暴力破解，并与使用巧妙的完美散列小心避免它们的实现在同一范围内着陆，这并不算太糟糕！ 我们的还有一个额外的好处，可以随意交换 Base64 字母表，而 vb64 的完美哈希则与标准 Base64 字母表绑定在一起。

不使用 SIMD 的生产实现运行速度为 2.7 GiB/s 编码和 2.3 GiB/s 解码，因此 SIMD 非常值得。 我们在 AVX-512 CPU 上的实现运行速度约为 13 GiB/s，而 vb64 几乎无法从 AVX-512 中受益。 最先进的 AVX-512 实现转换为安全 Rust，以 35 GiB/s 编码和 20 GiB/s 解码运行，因此我们的原型不是最佳的，但仍然让我们看到对单个操作的改进有利于更大的算法。

我已经在 Zen 4 工作站上的 fearless_simd 上运行了这些测量，因为 std::simd 的贡献指南没有说明如何对您的更改进行基准测试。

fearless_simd 更好吗？

对于这一特定操作，swizzle_dyn，是的。 现在 std::simd 和 fearless_simd 中的实现是相同的，但后者不需要 -Z build-std 并且可以很好地对该函数进行多版本化。

但是，当 std::simd 发挥其优势时，它确实很难被击败甚至匹敌。

例如，接受编译时已知索引的 std::simd::swizzle 会直接转换为 LLVM“洗牌向量”操作，并且 LLVM 从一长串特殊情况中选择最适合您的 CPU 的公式。

在稳定的 Rust 上复制这一点需要大量的工作，并且需要大量的 const 评估或程序宏，这两者都会对构建时间产生负面影响。 据我所知，还没有人尝试过这样做。

也就是说，LLVM 并不完美。 例如，我发现它通过将某些 AVX-512 shuffle 转变为大量 SSE 操作而不是仅仅将其保留为本机 AVX-512 shuffle 来破坏它们的性能。 我已将其报告为 Rust 中的一个问题，然后 Rust 标准库维护者将其报告给 LLVM 并尝试修复它，这导致 LLVM 开发人员做出了大量改进。 这项工作将在 LLVM 23 中发布。

我已经发现了 LLVM 的另一个低效率问题，但修复它的 PR 只会让 LLVM 陷入无限循环。 如果您正在寻找一个有趣的调试难题，这里有一个！

LLVM 问题会影响 std::simd、所有其他 SIMD 包，甚至 C 和 C++ 等其他语言，因此修复它们始终是一个巨大的胜利。

要点

这就是我学到的：

  • std::simd::swizzle_dyn 甚至没有它看起来那么有用，因为多版本控制对其不起作用。
  • 与实际开发状态相比，每晚的 std::simd 已经过时了。
  • std::simd 的贡献文档很差：很容易在错误的存储库中打开过时版本的 PR。没有记录的方法来反汇编或基准测试您的更改。
  • 即使您使用应该降低为特定指令的内部函数，LLVM 也可以破坏完美的最佳 SIMD 代码。 不仅在 Rust 中，在 C 和 C++ 中也是如此！

而且，一旦这项工作同步到标准库中，std::simd::swizzle_dyn 的速度将提高 6 倍。 不客气！
